title: Momentum — mass in motion
examTip: Momentum calculations are a reliable exam question — p = mv is straightforward but AQA also tests conservation of momentum (total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system) and the impulse-momentum relationship (F = Δp/t or FΔt = Δp). Always include units: kg m/s.
introTips: Momentum has direction as well as magnitude — it's a vector quantity. When things collide, momentum can be transferred from one object to another.
Conservation of momentum is the key principle: in any collision or explosion, total momentum before = total momentum after (provided no external forces).
Impulse = force × time = change in momentum. Larger force for shorter time, or smaller force for longer time, gives the same change in momentum.
intro: Momentum is the physics quantity that captures how hard it is to stop something that's moving. A lorry moving slowly can have the same momentum as a car moving fast — and stopping either requires the same effort. Momentum is always conserved in collisions and explosions, which makes it one of the most powerful tools in mechanics.

keyPoints.heading: Calculating momentum
keyPoints.content: Momentum is defined as the product of an object's mass and its velocity. The equation is: p = mv, where p is momentum (kg m/s), m is mass (kg), and v is velocity (m/s). Momentum is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. This means when two objects move in opposite directions, their momenta have opposite signs. Momentum is measured in kg m/s (or N s — these are equivalent units). Example: a 1,500 kg car travelling at 20 m/s has momentum = 1,500 × 20 = 30,000 kg m/s.
writeBullets: Units: kg m/s (or N s — equivalent)
Momentum is a vector — has direction as well as magnitude
Objects moving in opposite directions have opposite signs of momentum
Example: 1,500 kg car at 20 m/s → p = 1,500 × 20 = 30,000 kg m/s

keyTerms.def: The product of an object's mass and velocity: p = mv; measured in kg m/s; a vector quantity with direction
A quantity that has both magnitude (size) and direction — e.g. momentum, velocity, force

keyPoints.memTip: p = mv. The unit is kg m/s — same as mass × velocity units. Momentum is a vector — if you see two objects moving in opposite directions, one has positive momentum and the other negative. Keep track of direction in any conservation problem.

keyPoints.heading: Conservation of momentum
keyPoints.content: The law of conservation of momentum states that in a closed system (no external forces), the total momentum before an event equals the total momentum after. This applies to collisions and explosions. For a collision: m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂, where u is initial velocity and v is final velocity. For an explosion (e.g. a gun firing): before the event, total momentum = 0 (everything stationary). After the event, the bullet and gun move in opposite directions — their momenta are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign, so they still sum to zero. This is why a gun recoils when fired.
writeBullets: Conservation of momentum: total momentum before = total momentum after (closed system)
Collision equation: m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂
Explosion: before = 0; after = equal and opposite momenta (sum still = 0)
Gun recoil: bullet goes forward (+), gun recoils backward (−), total = 0

keyTerms.def: In a closed system (no external forces), the total momentum before an event equals the total momentum after
A system in which no external forces act, so momentum is conserved

keyPoints.memTip: Total momentum before = total momentum after. Always. Calculate total momentum on the left side (before), set it equal to the right side (after), and solve for the unknown. For explosions: before = 0, so after the two momenta must be equal and opposite.

keyPoints.heading: Force, impulse and change in momentum
keyPoints.content: A resultant force acting on an object changes its momentum. The relationship is: F = Δp / Δt, or rearranged: FΔt = Δp. This means force × time = change in momentum. The quantity FΔt is called the impulse. A large force for a short time produces the same change in momentum as a small force for a long time. This principle explains safety features: crumple zones and airbags extend the time of a collision, reducing the force experienced (same Δp, larger Δt → smaller F). Wearing a helmet in cycling or motorcycling works the same way.
writeBullets: F = Δp / Δt (force = change in momentum ÷ time)
Larger time → smaller force for same change in momentum
Crumple zones, airbags: increase collision time → reduce force on passengers
Helmets, padded flooring: same principle — extend time of impact

keyTerms.def: Force multiplied by time (FΔt) — equal to the change in momentum; measured in N s (equivalent to kg m/s)
The difference between final and initial momentum: Δp = mv − mu; caused by a resultant force acting over time

keyPoints.memTip: FΔt = Δp. Force × time = change in momentum. More time → less force for the same Δp. Safety devices: crumple zones, airbags, helmets all increase the TIME of impact → reduce the FORCE → less injury. AQA loves this application — always link the equation to a real example.

tables.title: Momentum equations summary
rows: Calculating momentum of a single object
Total momentum before = total momentum after
Force = rate of change of momentum
When force and time given; find change in momentum
Safety device questions; comparing force and time

0.question: A 2 kg ball travelling at 5 m/s collides with a stationary 3 kg ball. They stick together. What is their combined velocity after the collision?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — total momentum before = 2 × 5 + 3 × 0 = 10 kg m/s. After: (2 + 3) × v = 10, so v = 10/5 = 2 m/s.
0.wrongFeedback: Use conservation of momentum: total before = total after. Before: p = 2 × 5 + 3 × 0 = 10 kg m/s. After: the combined mass is 2 + 3 = 5 kg, and momentum is still 10 kg m/s. So v = 10 ÷ 5 = 2 m/s. The velocity is less because the total mass increased.

2.question: Why do crumple zones in cars reduce injury in a crash?
options: They reduce the total change in momentum during the collision
They increase the time of the collision, which reduces the force experienced by passengers
They absorb kinetic energy and convert it to electrical energy
They increase the mass of the car, reducing deceleration
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — F = Δp/Δt. The change in momentum is fixed (same speed change). By increasing the time (Δt), the force (F) is reduced. Less force = less injury.
2.wrongFeedback: The change in momentum (Δp) in a crash is fixed — the car stops from a given speed. Crumple zones increase the TIME (Δt) over which the car decelerates. Using F = Δp/Δt: same Δp, larger Δt → smaller F. Smaller force on passengers → less injury. The total momentum change isn't reduced — it's the force that's reduced.

commonMistakes: Forgetting momentum is a vector — in conservation problems where objects move in opposite directions, one must be assigned a negative velocity. Getting this wrong gives the wrong answer.
Confusing impulse (FΔt) with work (Fd) — impulse involves time, work involves distance. Different concepts, don't mix them.
In conservation of momentum calculations, adding masses instead of using them separately — for a collision where objects stick together, add the masses AFTER the collision, not before.
Saying crumple zones 'reduce momentum' — they don't. They increase the time of impact to reduce the force. The momentum change is the same.
examTips: For momentum calculation questions: (1) state p = mv, (2) calculate momentum before the event, (3) apply conservation, (4) solve for unknown. Show all steps.
For 'explain why crumple zones reduce injury': quote F = Δp/Δt, state that Δp is fixed, explain that crumple zones increase Δt, conclude that F decreases. This is the full mark scheme answer.
When objects move in opposite directions, assign one velocity as negative. This is critical for explosion questions where initial momentum = 0.
Units check: momentum is kg m/s or N s (equivalent). Impulse is also N s. Force is N, time is s — so N × s = N s = kg m/s. All consistent.
revisionCardBullets: Momentum: p = mv (mass × velocity); units = kg m/s
Momentum is a vector — has direction; opposite directions = opposite signs
Conservation of momentum: total before = total after (closed system)
Explosion: before p = 0; after: two objects move in opposite directions with equal magnitude momenta
Force-momentum: F = Δp / Δt (force = change in momentum ÷ time)
Impulse: FΔt = Δp (force × time = change in momentum)
Safety devices (crumple zones, airbags, helmets): increase collision time → reduce force
Same Δp, larger Δt → smaller F → less injury
